We need more granular permissions so editors can't accidentally publish drafts.
We added guardrails in the CI pipeline to block direct commits to main.
The hardcoded URL in the config broke everything when the server moved.
Just a heads-up — the deploy is happening at three today, not five.
Give me a high-level overview first; we can dig into the details later.
The impact of the change was a twelve percent reduction in support tickets.
The implication of removing that field is that old clients will break.
Let's step back — we're getting in the weeds and losing the bigger picture.
New feature requests go through an intake process before joining the backlog.
Avoid jargon in the release notes — not everyone knows what a webhook is.
The project kick-off is set for Monday morning with all stakeholders present.
We can leverage the existing design system instead of building a new one.
Let's tackle the low-hanging fruit first to show quick wins to the stakeholders.
Reaching the first public beta is a major milestone for the whole team.
I'll mark this as a nitpick so it doesn't block the review from being approved.
Our north star metric is the number of active users after thirty days.
There's still an open loop on who owns the migration — can we close that today?
The framework is very opinionated, which makes onboarding faster but limits flexibility.
The approval process adds two days of overhead to every small change.
Who owns this feature once it ships — product or engineering?
The biggest pain point for users is that logging in takes too many steps.
Always leave a paper trail for decisions made in Slack by copying them to Jira.
That's a great point — let's put it in the parking lot for the next meeting.
After the user research, we decided to pivot and focus on the mobile experience.
The postmortem revealed that a missing timeout caused the outage.
A pre-mortem helped us spot three risks we hadn't considered in the plan.
The engineering team gave pushback on the tight timeline for the migration.
I went down a rabbit hole debugging the wrong file for an hour.
The new engineer will need about two weeks to fully ramp-up on the codebase.
Please add the rationale to the PR description so reviewers understand the why.
We'll refine the estimates once we have more information from the API team.
In the retrospective, the team agreed that daily standups were taking too long.
The legal review is the main roadblock keeping us from launching next week.
The rollout will be gradual — ten percent of users this week, the rest next week.
I found the bug by rubber duck debugging out loud to my teammate.
Can you do a quick sanity check on these numbers before I send them to the exec?
The project doubled in size due to scope creep from last-minute feature requests.
We need sign-off from the security team before the data goes into production.
We did a one-day spike to see if the third-party API would meet our needs.
The retro was a good time to surface the tension that had been building in the team.
She gave tactful feedback that was honest without making anyone defensive.
Technical debt from the initial prototype is slowing down every new feature.
Let's time-box this discussion to fifteen minutes and then make a decision.
The beta launched last week and is already getting traction with early users.
The main trade-off is between a faster build time and a larger bundle size.
We triage bug reports every Monday to separate critical issues from minor ones.
High turnover means a lot of institutional knowledge leaves with every person.
I'll unblock you as soon as I finish reviewing the PR — give me an hour.
Cut the verbosity in the error message — users don't need the full stack trace.
Better visibility into the deploy status would help the whole team.
The workaround fixes the crash for now, but the root cause still needs attention.
I started yak shaving when I tried to update one package and ended up refactoring the config.

technical-writing.exampleSentence: Use active voice to make every instruction clear and direct for the reader.
Add a warning admonition before the step that deletes user data.
Anchoring the link to a specific section saves the reader from scrolling.
The API reference lists every parameter, its type, and an example value.
Strong audience awareness means you don't explain git to senior engineers.
Put the security warning in a callout so it stands out from the rest of the text.
Every changelog entry should say what changed and why the user should care.
A good code comment explains the why, not the what — the code already shows the what.
Always use a code fence with a language tag so the syntax gets highlighted.
Avoid colloquialisms in docs that will be translated or read by non-native speakers.
Conciseness means cutting every word that doesn't add information for the reader.
Decide the content type before writing — is this a tutorial or a how-to guide?
Add a cross-reference to the authentication guide at the start of this section.
Add a deprecation notice to warn users before removing the old endpoint.
We restructured the docs using the Diátaxis framework to separate tutorials from references.
A disambiguation note clarifies which definition of model applies in this context.
A docstring at the top of the function explains the parameters and return type.
Docs-as-code means every change to the docs goes through a pull request review.
Good edge case documentation tells users what the system does with empty inputs.
Write evergreen content that stays accurate without needing constant updates.
An explainer helps readers understand why JWT authentication works the way it does.
The front matter at the top of the file sets the page title and description.
Link unfamiliar terms to the glossary so readers can look them up immediately.
A how-to guide walks you through setting up authentication in your app.
Use the imperative mood in steps: click, open, run, not you should click.
Strong information scent means users can predict what's behind every link.
Format the variable name with inline code so it stands out from the surrounding text.
JSDoc comments on exported functions are picked up automatically by the IDE.
The knowledge base gets fifty percent of its traffic from search engines.
Good localization adapts date formats and units, not just the language.
Put the key information in the lede so skimmers get the point immediately.
All our documentation is written in Markdown and stored alongside the code.
MDX lets us embed a live code playground directly inside the documentation page.
Good microcopy on the error message tells users exactly how to fix the problem.
A clear navigational label sets the right expectation before the user clicks.
User account management interface settings page is a classic noun pile — rewrite it.
Strong onboarding documentation gets new users to their first success in minutes.
The OpenAPI spec is the single source of truth for all our API endpoints.
The form was submitted is passive voice — submitted the form is clearer.
Plain language guidelines say to avoid words with shorter, clearer alternatives.
List every prerequisite at the top so users don't get stuck halfway through.
Progressive disclosure hides advanced options until the user is ready for them.
A short paragraph of prose before the steps gives useful context without slowing readers down.
The README is the first thing a new contributor reads, so keep it focused.
Release notes should highlight what changed for users, not for engineers.
Follow the runbook step by step when you get paged for this kind of incident.
Scannability improves when you use short paragraphs, headings, and bold key terms.
Single sourcing means you update the authentication guide once and it updates everywhere.
A good code snippet is short enough to copy and run without modification.
Write the spec before the code so everyone agrees on what is being built.
The style guide says to use second person and present tense throughout the docs.
A table of contents at the top helps readers jump straight to what they need.
Technical accuracy means every code sample in the docs has been tested to work.
Consistent terminology across the docs prevents confusion about the same concept.
The TOC is auto-generated from headings so it stays up to date automatically.
The tone of voice in error messages should be helpful, never condescending.
A tutorial guides a beginner from zero to a working example by the end.
Good UX writing on the empty state tells users exactly what to do next.
Versioned documentation lets users of older releases find the right information.
The product's voice is warm and direct, never robotic or overly formal.
The onboarding walkthrough shows new users the three most important features.
Remove the word simply — it's a weasel word that makes users feel bad when they struggle.
Word economy means every sentence earns its place on the page.

ux-design.exampleSentence: A raised button has the affordance of something that can be pressed.
The anatomy diagram breaks the card component into its labeled parts.
Add an annotation to explain the hover state before sending the design to dev.
Good accessibility means the product works for everyone, not just most people.
An affordance map helped us find interactive elements that weren't obvious enough.
Card sorting showed that users grouped settings very differently than we expected.
Chunking the form into three steps made the process feel less overwhelming.
Every extra option on the screen adds to the user's cognitive load.
The contrast ratio between the text and background fails the WCAG AA standard.
A clear content hierarchy guides the eye from the headline to the call to action.
Desirability testing showed users found the dark theme more professional.
Years of design debt meant every page had a slightly different button style.
A clean design handoff means engineers don't have to guess the spacing values.
A good design critique focuses on the problem first, not the proposed solution.
Low discoverability means users find the feature by accident, not by looking.
What does the empty state look like? That's an important edge case to design.
The empty state should explain what goes here and how to add the first item.
Every form field needs an error state that tells the user exactly what's wrong.
Start with low fidelity to explore ideas quickly before committing to details.
Fitts's law explains why the submit button should be large and close to the form.
Map the flow from landing page to checkout before designing any single screen.
Gestalt principles explain why elements close together feel like they belong together.
Use a ghost button for secondary actions so the primary action stands out more.
The golden ratio can guide proportions in a layout to feel naturally balanced.
The twelve-column grid system keeps every layout consistent across the product.
Guerrilla testing in a coffee shop gave us five insights in under an hour.
Jakob Nielsen's ten heuristics are a good starting point for any UI review.
A heuristic evaluation found three serious usability problems before any user testing.
Strong visual hierarchy draws the eye to the most important action on the page.
Add a hotspot to the menu icon so testers can navigate in the prototype.
Poor information architecture was why users couldn't find their account settings.
Interaction design defines how the panel animates in and what triggers it.
Iterative design got us from a rough idea to a tested solution in two weeks.
The jobs-to-be-done framework helped us see that users hire the app to save time.
The journey map revealed a frustrating gap between signup and the first real value.
High learnability means users can do basic tasks correctly on their first try.
Match the UI to the user's mental model of how folders and files should work.
A small microinteraction on the save button confirms the action without interrupting.
Use a modal for destructive actions so the user must consciously confirm.
The MVP had only three features but was enough to validate the core idea.
A tab bar is the right navigation pattern for apps with four to five main sections.
Smooth onboarding is the single biggest predictor of long-term retention.
The biggest pain point users described was having to re-enter the same data twice.
Participatory design sessions gave users a voice in shaping the new dashboard.
The primary persona is a project manager who checks the tool three times a day.
Progressive disclosure hides advanced settings until the user asks for them.
We built a clickable prototype before writing a single line of production code.
Proximity groups the label and input so the relationship is immediately clear.
The checkout is a red route — nothing should block or slow it down.
Responsive design means the layout adapts gracefully from desktop to phone.
The scaffold guides new users until they're comfortable enough to explore on their own.
Good scannability means users find what they're looking for without reading everything.
High serviceability means the team can patch and update the product quickly.
The underline on a link is a signifier telling users they can click it.
A skeleton screen keeps the layout stable while the content is still loading.
Skeuomorphism made the early iPhone apps look like real-world objects on purpose.
The sticky element keeps the navigation bar visible as the user scrolls down.
Map the task flow for resetting a password before designing any single screen.
The think-aloud protocol revealed that users were confused by the icon's meaning.
A toast message in the corner confirms the file was saved without interrupting the user.
A tooltip explains what the icon button does when the user hovers over it.
Tree testing showed that most users looked for billing under Account, not Settings.
Five sessions of usability testing surfaced the three most critical problems.
The user flow diagram maps every path from signup to the first successful action.
Write a user story for each feature so the team knows who it's for and why.
A feature with low utility that is hard to use will not survive the next redesign.
Inline validation tells users about a problem before they submit the whole form.
The primary button has more visual weight than the secondary one to stand out.
Breadcrumbs are a classic wayfinding tool that shows users where they are.
Every color combination in the UI needs to pass the WCAG AA contrast ratio.
More whitespace around the headline made the page feel cleaner and more focused.
A wireframe focuses on layout and content without getting distracted by color.
A wizard breaks the complex account setup into six manageable steps.
The zero state is the perfect moment to guide users to their first action.
The hero section uses a Z-pattern layout to lead the eye toward the CTA.
F-pattern reading means the most important content should sit on the left side.
Put the main call to action above the fold so it's visible without scrolling.
A breadcrumb trail helps users navigate back from a deep page in the hierarchy.
The call to action should be the most visually prominent element on the page.
A pre-checked newsletter box is a common dark pattern in signup flows.
A short feedback loop between users and the team keeps the product on the right track.
